conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the eligibility rules. To help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Now, does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, it's a bit complicated. Alex has been in care with the local authority for a few months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, thank you for sharing that. To help me assess your eligibility properly, I need to ask a few more questions about Alex's situation.

How long has Alex been in care - is it more or less than 8 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been more than 8 weeks - about 12 weeks now, I think.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. This is an important detail. 

Does Alex come home to stay with you for any regular periods? For example, does he spend at least 24 hours a week at home, or does he stay with you for at least 2 nights in a row each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't come home regularly like that. He's at the care home full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. One more important question: do you regularly spend money on Alex - for example, on things like clothes, presents, food, or pocket money?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to contribute financially while he's been in care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the rules I've reviewed, I need to give you the outcome of this assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Result:**

I'm sorry to inform you that based on your circumstances, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

According to the Child Benefit rules, when a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks, you usually stop being entitled to Child Benefit. Since Alex has been in care for approximately 12 weeks, this exceeds the 8-week limit.

There are some exceptions that would allow you to continue receiving Child Benefit even after 8 weeks in care, such as:
- If the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, OR
- If you regularly spend money on the child (for things like clothes, presents, food, or pocket money)

Unfortunately, in your situation, neither of these exceptions apply as Alex doesn't come home regularly and you haven't been able to make financial contributions while he's been in care.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex starts coming home regularly or if you begin contributing financially to his upkeep - you may want to contact HMRC to discuss whether you could become eligible again.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: According to the Child Benefit rules for children in care, when a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks, you are usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit. There are exceptions to this rule - for example, if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or if you regularly spend money on them. In Alex's case: (1) He has been in care for approximately 12 weeks, which exceeds the 8-week limit; (2) He does not come home regularly for at least 24 hours per week or 2 nights in a row each week; (3) You are not regularly spending money on him while he's in care. Therefore, none of the exceptions apply, and you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex under these circumstances.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child (Alex, age 8) who has been in local authority care for approximately 12 weeks. The claimant lives in the UK but does not have the child living with them, the child does not visit home regularly, and the claimant is not making financial contributions toward the child's upkeep. Under the rules for children in care, Child Benefit entitlement typically ends after 8 weeks unless specific exceptions apply. Since none of these exceptions are met in this case, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

There are exceptions to this rule - for example, if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or if you regularly spend money on them. In Alex's case: (1) He has been in care for approximately 12 weeks, which exceeds the 8-week limit; (2) He does not come home regularly for at least 24 hours per week or 2 nights in a row each week; (3) You are not regularly spending money on him while he's in care. Therefore, none of the exceptions apply, and you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex under these circumstances.

The claimant lives in the UK but does not have the child living with them, the child does not visit home regularly, and the claimant is not making financial contributions toward the child's upkeep. Under the rules for children in care, Child Benefit entitlement typically ends after 8 weeks unless specific exceptions apply. Since none of these exceptions are met in this case, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
